summary: Gruber argues that CES has devolved into a parade of concept designs rather than real products, building on Dieter Bohn's observation that most exciting announcements at CES 2020 lacked ship dates. He disagrees with Bohn's framing that concepts reflect a 'lack of confidence,' instead asserting they reflect an inability to design real products and a dereliction of the actual duties of design. He invokes Steve Jobs's 'Parable of the Concept Car' to illustrate that true design means working within real-world constraints, not fantasizing without them. Gruber revisits Apple's own cautionary tale — the 1987 Knowledge Navigator video — as a warning sign of leadership untethered from reality. He endorses Kontra's law: a company's innovation is inversely proportional to its tendency to publicly release concept products.
key_points: CES has become a showcase for concept designs rather than shippable products, with foldables, cars, and other announcements lacking concrete ship dates
Concept designs are not a sign of lacking confidence but of companies that are bad at designing actual products — they represent a dereliction of design's real duties
True design means working within real-world constraints like engineering, pricing, manufacturing, and physics — concepts allow designers to ignore all of these
Apple's Knowledge Navigator video from 1987 was an early warning sign of leadership disconnected from reality, foreshadowing the product stagnation that nearly killed the company
Publicly showing concepts gives competitors a head start and makes eventual real products feel like old news
money_quotes: Concept designs (and worse, concept videos) are a sign of dysfunction and incompetence at a company. It's playing make-believe while fooling yourself and your audience into thinking you're doing something real.

Concepts allow designers to ignore real-world constraints: engineering, pricing, manufacturing, legal regulations, sometimes even physics. But dealing with real-world constraints is the hard work of true design.
Concepts don't stem from a lack of confidence. They stem from a dereliction of the actual duties of design.
Either it can be made, or it can't. If it can, show it when it's real.
Designing at the limits of possibility is one thing; designing unbounded by reality is another.
What a pile of pie-in-the-sky horseshit the Knowledge Navigator video was — and in most ways would remain so even if it were released today.
I'm arguing that the Knowledge Navigator was a warning — a dead canary in the coal mine — that Apple's leadership wasn't focused on the present, and probably not even focused on reality.
A commercial company's ability to innovate is inversely proportional to its proclivity to publicly release conceptual products.
key_insight: Publicly showcasing concept designs is not visionary leadership but a failure of actual design discipline — real innovation means shipping products that solve hard engineering and manufacturing constraints, not fantasizing without them.
